title: My First Swimming Lesson
summary: A child describes his very first swimming lesson at the local pool — from meeting the teacher to learning to float for the first time.
body: Last Saturday, I had my first swimming lesson at the local pool. I was very excited but also a little nervous. The pool was big and the water looked cold and blue. My swimming teacher was a tall woman named Coach Sarah. She had a kind voice and a loud whistle around her neck. First, she showed us how to hold the side of the pool. Then she taught us to kick our legs in the water. The water splashed everywhere and we all laughed. After that, Coach Sarah gave each of us a float board. We held the board and kicked our legs to move across the pool. I was surprised because I could actually move through the water. At the end of the lesson, she showed us how to put our face in the water and blow bubbles. It felt strange at first, but I did it! I felt very proud of myself. My mum was sitting in the viewing area and she waved at me. I cannot wait for my next lesson.

annotations.meaningVi: hồ bơi địa phương, gần nhà
annotations.exampleEn: They go to the local pool every Sunday morning.
She was nervous before her first driving lesson.
The referee blew his whistle to stop the game.
You need to kick your legs to move forward in the water.
The children splashed water at each other in the pool.
Beginners use a float board to practise kicking.
He was surprised to see his friend at the airport.
The swimming teacher asked the children to blow bubbles underwater.
She felt proud after finishing the race.

annotations.meaningVi: khu vực ngồi xem (cho phụ huynh)
annotations.exampleEn: Parents sat in the viewing area to watch the swimming lesson.
I thought it would be hard, but it was actually quite easy.

annotations.meaningVi: không thể chờ được, rất háo hức
annotations.exampleEn: I cannot wait to see you next week!

grammarNotes.title: Sequencing Words: First, Then, After that, At the end
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Các từ như 'First' (Đầu tiên), 'Then' (Sau đó), 'After that' (Tiếp theo), và 'At the end' (Cuối cùng) giúp sắp xếp các sự kiện theo thứ tự thời gian. Trong bài này, chúng mô tả từng bước của buổi học bơi. Khi kể chuyện, hãy dùng những từ này để câu chuyện dễ theo dõi hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'First', 'Then', 'After that', and 'At the end' are sequencing words that show the order of events. They help listeners follow a story step by step. Example: 'First, she showed us how to hold the side. Then she taught us to kick our legs.' Use these when telling a story or explaining a process.

grammarNotes.title: Past Simple with Irregular Verbs
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài này có nhiều động từ bất quy tắc ở thì quá khứ: show → showed (thông thường), teach → taught, give → gave, feel → felt, sit → sat, have → had. Hãy chú ý đặc biệt đến những dạng này vì chúng xuất hiện rất thường xuyên trong tiếng Anh hằng ngày.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: This story uses several irregular past verbs: teach → taught, give → gave, feel → felt, sit → sat, have → had. These common verbs do not follow the regular '-ed' pattern and must be memorised. They appear very frequently in everyday English.

translationVi: Thứ Bảy tuần trước, tôi có buổi học bơi đầu tiên tại hồ bơi gần nhà. Tôi rất hào hứng nhưng cũng hơi hồi hộp. Hồ bơi rất rộng và nước trông lạnh và xanh màu. Giáo viên dạy bơi của tôi là một người phụ nữ cao, tên là Huấn luyện viên Sarah. Cô ấy có giọng nói nhẹ nhàng và một chiếc còi to đeo trước ngực. Đầu tiên, cô ấy chỉ cho chúng tôi cách bám vào thành hồ. Sau đó cô dạy chúng tôi đạp chân trong nước. Nước bắn tóe khắp nơi và chúng tôi đều cười phá lên. Tiếp theo, Huấn luyện viên Sarah phát cho mỗi người một tấm phao. Chúng tôi cầm phao và đạp chân để bơi qua hồ. Tôi ngạc nhiên vì mình thực sự có thể di chuyển qua mặt nước. Cuối buổi học, cô ấy chỉ chúng tôi cách cho mặt vào nước và thổi bong bóng. Lúc đầu cảm giác thật kỳ lạ, nhưng tôi đã làm được! Tôi cảm thấy rất tự hào về bản thân. Mẹ tôi đang ngồi ở khu vực dành cho phụ huynh và vẫy tay với tôi. Tôi không thể chờ đến buổi học tiếp theo.

questions.explanation: The story says 'Last Saturday, I had my first swimming lesson.'
questions.hint: Which day of the week was the lesson?

questions.prompt: How did the child feel before the lesson?
questions.explanation: The story says 'I was very excited but also a little nervous.'
questions.hint: The child had two feelings — what were they?

questions.prompt: What was the swimming teacher's name?
questions.explanation: The story says 'My swimming teacher was a tall woman named Coach Sarah.'
questions.hint: What name does the story give the teacher?

questions.prompt: What was around the teacher's neck?
questions.explanation: The story says 'a loud whistle around her neck.'
questions.hint: What tool does a sports coach usually carry?

questions.prompt: What was the FIRST thing the teacher showed the class?
options: How to hold the side of the pool
questions.explanation: The story says 'First, she showed us how to hold the side of the pool.'
questions.hint: What does 'First' signal in the story?

questions.prompt: What happened when the children kicked their legs?
options: They swam to the other side
questions.explanation: The story says 'The water splashed everywhere and we all laughed.'
questions.hint: What did the water do when they kicked?

questions.prompt: What did the teacher give each child to help them move across the pool?
questions.explanation: The story says 'Coach Sarah gave each of us a float board.'
questions.hint: What equipment helped them move through the water?

questions.prompt: What was the last thing they practised?
options: Putting their face in water and blowing bubbles
questions.explanation: The story says 'At the end of the lesson, she showed us how to put our face in the water and blow bubbles.'
questions.hint: What activity is described 'at the end of the lesson'?

questions.prompt: How did blowing bubbles feel to the child at first?
questions.explanation: The story says 'It felt strange at first.'
questions.hint: What word describes his initial feeling about putting his face in the water?

questions.prompt: How did the child feel after the lesson?
questions.explanation: The story says 'I felt very proud of myself.'
questions.hint: What emotion does the child feel about his achievement?

questions.prompt: Where was the child's mum during the lesson?
questions.explanation: The story says 'My mum was sitting in the viewing area.'
questions.hint: Where do parents watch swim lessons from?

cloze.template: Last Saturday, I had my first swimming lesson at the local pool. I was very excited but also a little {{b1}}. The pool was big and the water looked cold and blue. My swimming teacher was a tall woman named Coach Sarah. She had a kind voice and a loud {{b2}} around her neck. First, she showed us how to hold the side of the pool. Then she taught us to {{b3}} our legs in the water. The water {{b4}} everywhere and we all laughed. After that, Coach Sarah gave each of us a {{b5}}. We held the board and kicked our legs to move across the pool. I was {{b6}} because I could actually move through the water. At the end of the lesson, she showed us how to put our face in the water and blow {{b7}}. It felt strange at first, but I did it! I felt very {{b8}} of myself. My mum was sitting in the {{b9}} area and she waved at me. I cannot {{b10}} for my next lesson.

blanks.explanation: 'Nervous' means feeling anxious or worried — common before trying something new.
A 'whistle' is used by coaches and referees to give signals.
'Kick your legs' is a basic swimming technique — pushing your feet up and down in the water.
'Splashed' means the water flew up and spread out — typical when kicking in a pool.
A 'float board' is a flat foam board that helps beginner swimmers stay above the water.
'Surprised' means he didn't expect it — he was amazed that he could move in the water.
'Blow bubbles' means breathing out through your mouth underwater — an early swimming skill.
'Proud' means feeling pleased with yourself after achieving something.
The 'viewing area' is the section of the swimming centre where parents sit to watch lessons.
'Cannot wait' is an idiom meaning very eager and excited about something coming soon.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The child was nervous before the lesson but felt proud at the end. Can you think of a time when you were afraid to try something new but it turned out well? What helped you get through the fear?
